statewide.description: The Governor is Vermont's chief executive, responsible for managing state government, signing or vetoing legislation, appointing judges and cabinet members, and representing Vermont in national affairs.
responsibilities: Signs or vetoes legislation passed by the General Assembly
Appoints members of the Vermont Supreme Court and other judges
Manages executive branch state agencies and departments
Proposes the state budget each year
Activates the Vermont National Guard in emergencies
statewide.whyItMatters: The Governor is Vermont's top executive, setting the state's policy agenda and either signing or vetoing legislation. Gov. Scott has vetoed several major bills passed by Democratic legislative supermajorities, including proposals on housing, taxes, and social policy. Vermont elects its Governor every two years with no term limits.

incumbents.name: Phil Scott
incumbents.statusNote: Has not formally announced but widely expected to run for a sixth two-year term
incumbents.bio: Phil Scott has served as Vermont's Governor since January 2017, winning five consecutive two-year terms. A former contractor and racing driver, Scott is a Republican who has supported abortion rights and signed gun safety legislation while vetoing several Democratic tax and spending proposals. He won re-election in 2024 by more than 30 percentage points.
notablePositions: Supports abortion rights
Has signed gun safety legislation
Has vetoed several Democratic tax and spending proposals
Led Vermont's COVID-19 vaccine rollout, one of the fastest in the nation
Focus on housing affordability and economic development

statewide.notes: No major Democratic challenger has announced as of March 2026. Attorney General Charity Clark and Treasurer Mike Pieciak have both been mentioned in press reports as potential candidates but have both signaled preference for reelection in their current offices. Candidate filing opens April 27, 2026. (Sources: Seven Days, VTDigger)

statewide.description: The Lieutenant Governor presides over the Vermont Senate (but only votes to break ties), serves on several state boards, and becomes Governor if the Governor is unable to serve.
responsibilities: Presides over the Vermont State Senate
Succeeds the Governor if the Governor is unable to serve
Serves on the Governor's Commission on Women and other state boards
Advocates on various policy issues
statewide.whyItMatters: Vermont elects its Governor and Lieutenant Governor separately — they can and often do come from different parties. The Lt. Governor has limited formal executive power but plays an important succession role and can shape the Senate's proceedings.

incumbents.name: John Rodgers
incumbents.bio: John Rodgers has served as Lieutenant Governor since 2021. He previously served in the Vermont House and Senate representing the Northeast Kingdom. Rodgers has publicly stated he supports Second Amendment rights. He won reelection in 2024.
notablePositions: Supports Second Amendment rights while serving as a Democrat
Focus on rural Vermont issues and the Northeast Kingdom
Advocates for workforce development and economic opportunity

statewide.notes: Lt. Gov. Rodgers announced he is running for reelection.

statewide.description: The Attorney General is Vermont's chief law enforcement officer, representing the state in legal matters, enforcing consumer protection laws, and prosecuting public corruption.
responsibilities: Represents Vermont in all legal proceedings
Enforces consumer protection and antitrust laws
Investigates public corruption and Medicaid fraud
Issues legal opinions on constitutional questions
Coordinates with law enforcement on criminal justice issues
statewide.whyItMatters: The Attorney General represents Vermont's interests in court, enforces consumer protection and environmental laws, and can join multi-state legal actions on issues like opioids and corporate fraud. The office also handles criminal appeals and civil rights matters.

incumbents.name: Charity Clark
incumbents.statusNote: Has indicated preference for reelection over a gubernatorial run
incumbents.bio: Charity Clark was elected Vermont Attorney General in 2022, becoming the first woman to hold the office. She previously served as Chief Assistant Attorney General. She has been active in multi-state coalitions on opioids, environmental protection, and consumer fraud.
notablePositions: First woman to serve as Vermont Attorney General
Active in multi-state opioid and environmental enforcement actions
Focus on consumer protection and corporate accountability
Considered as potential gubernatorial candidate; leaning toward AG reelection

statewide.notes: AG Clark has said she plans to run for a statewide office in 2026 and is leaning toward reelection as Attorney General.

statewide.description: The Secretary of State oversees Vermont's elections, maintains official state records, regulates professional licenses, and registers businesses and nonprofits.
responsibilities: Administers all Vermont state elections
Maintains the statewide voter registration system
Regulates professional and occupational licensing for 60+ professions
Registers corporations, LLCs, and nonprofits
Maintains official legislative and executive records
statewide.whyItMatters: The Secretary of State plays a direct role in how Vermont elections are run — including voter registration, absentee ballots, and the certification of results. Vermont consistently ranks among the easiest states in the nation to vote in.

incumbents.name: Sarah Copeland Hanzas
incumbents.bio: Sarah Copeland Hanzas was first elected Secretary of State in 2022. She previously served in the Vermont House of Representatives for over a decade. She succeeded Jim Condos, who served as Secretary of State from 2011–2022.
notablePositions: Expanded access to absentee voting
Modernized business registration systems
Advocates for election security and voter access

statewide.notes: Secretary Copeland Hanzas announced she is running for reelection.

statewide.description: The State Treasurer manages Vermont's finances, investments, and public debt, and administers retirement funds for state employees and teachers.
responsibilities: Manages Vermont's cash flow and investments (~$3 billion portfolio)
Administers Vermont State Employees' and Teachers' Retirement Systems
Issues state bonds and manages public debt
Oversees the unclaimed property program
Administers Vermont's ABLE savings accounts for people with disabilities
statewide.whyItMatters: Vermont's pension systems are a long-term fiscal challenge — the Treasurer plays a key role in managing unfunded liabilities and ensuring retirement security for thousands of state workers and teachers.

incumbents.name: Mike Pieciak
incumbents.statusNote: Has signaled preference for reelection over running for Governor
incumbents.bio: Mike Pieciak was first elected State Treasurer in 2022. He previously served as Commissioner of the Vermont Department of Financial Regulation and oversaw Vermont's pandemic stimulus distribution before becoming Treasurer.
notablePositions: Focus on fiscal responsibility and pension fund management
Advocates for Vermont housing investment programs
Widely considered a potential future gubernatorial candidate

statewide.notes: Treasurer Pieciak is widely expected to seek reelection, having signaled preference for his current office over a gubernatorial run in 2026.

statewide.description: The Auditor of Accounts independently audits Vermont state agencies, ensuring taxpayer money is spent efficiently, legally, and as intended. The Auditor reports to the legislature and the public — not the Governor.
responsibilities: Conducts performance and financial audits of state agencies and programs
Investigates waste, fraud, and abuse of public funds
Reports findings to the legislature and the public
Operates independently of both the Governor and legislature
Certifies state financial statements
statewide.whyItMatters: Vermont's Auditor of Accounts operates independently of both the Governor and the legislature, auditing state agencies and programs and reporting findings to the public. Six-term incumbent Doug Hoffer is retiring after 14 years, making this an open seat race.

incumbents.name: Doug Hoffer
incumbents.statusNote: Announced he will not seek reelection after six terms
incumbents.bio: Doug Hoffer served as Vermont Auditor of Accounts from 2013–2026, winning six consecutive terms. He announced before the 2024 election that 2026 would be his last term.
notablePositions: Audited state health benefit exchange, Agency of Transportation, and other major programs
Published detailed reports on Vermont housing and workforce issues
Served six consecutive terms (2013–2026)

knownCandidates.name: Tim Ashe
knownCandidates.bio: Tim Ashe served as Vermont Senate President Pro Tem from 2013–2021, one of the most powerful positions in Vermont state government. He ran for Lieutenant Governor in 2020, losing in the Democratic primary. He teaches at the University of Vermont and is a former Progressive-Democrat.
notablePositions: Former Vermont Senate President Pro Tem (2013–2021)
Led major Vermont policy initiatives on housing, climate, and tax reform
Ran for Lt. Governor in 2020 (lost Democratic primary)

statewide.notes: This is the most notable open seat in the 2026 Vermont elections. Tim Ashe (D) is the first major announced candidate. Additional candidates from both parties expected after filing opens April 27.

federal.description: Vermont has a single at-large congressional seat in the U.S. House of Representatives. One representative covers the entire state, serving on congressional committees, voting on federal legislation, and directing federal funding to Vermont.
responsibilities: Votes on all federal legislation in the U.S. House of Representatives
Represents Vermont on House committees
Helps secure federal appropriations for Vermont projects and programs
Constituent services: assists Vermonters with federal agency issues
Introduces and co-sponsors federal legislation
federal.whyItMatters: Vermont's sole House member plays an outsize role on federal matters affecting the state — including farm bills, defense appropriations, climate legislation, and social programs. Vermont has only one vote in the 435-member House, making that vote's alignment with Vermont's interests critical.

incumbents.name: Becca Balint
incumbents.bio: Becca Balint was elected Vermont's at-large U.S. Representative in 2022, becoming the first woman and first openly gay person elected to Congress from Vermont. She previously served in the Vermont Senate and as Senate President Pro Tem. She serves on the House Judiciary Committee and House Education and Workforce Committee.
notablePositions: First woman and first openly LGBTQ+ person elected to Congress from Vermont
Former Vermont Senate President Pro Tem
Serves on House Judiciary Committee
Focus on housing, climate, and workers' rights
Cook Political Report rates her seat as 'Solid Democratic'

federal.notes: Balint is expected to win easily. Vermont's at-large seat has been held by a Democrat or Independent since 1991.

stateSenate.description: The Vermont State Senate has 30 members who serve 2-year terms. The Chittenden Southeast district elects 3 senators, who pass state legislation, approve the state budget, and confirm the Governor's key appointments.
responsibilities: Passes Vermont state legislation in partnership with the House
Approves the state budget
Confirms Governor's appointments to key positions and boards
Ratifies constitutional amendments (must pass legislature twice before going to voters)
Provides constituent services for local communities
stateSenate.whyItMatters: Vermont's legislature has passed major legislation on housing, climate, gun safety, and education in recent sessions. The Senate has been at the center of high-profile fights with Governor Scott over tax policy and spending. Chittenden County's delegation has historically been among the most progressive in the state.

incumbents.name: Thomas Chittenden
incumbents.bio: Thomas Chittenden grew up in South Burlington and previously served on the South Burlington City Council. He was elected to the Vermont Senate representing Chittenden Southeast and is directly connected to his South Burlington constituents.
notablePositions: Former South Burlington City Councilor
Grew up in South Burlington
Serves on Senate committees

incumbents.name: Virginia Lyons
incumbents.bio: Virginia Lyons is a Vermont State Senator representing the Chittenden Southeast district. She has served in the Senate for multiple terms.
notablePositions: Experienced Senate member
Chittenden Southeast representative

incumbents.name: Kesha Ram Hinsdale
incumbents.bio: Kesha Ram Hinsdale is a Vermont State Senator who previously served as chair of the Vermont Progressive Party before becoming a Democrat. She served in the Vermont House before moving to the Senate.
notablePositions: Leading progressive voice in the Vermont Senate
Former Vermont Progressive Party chair
Focus on climate, housing, and racial equity

stateSenate.notes: All three seats are up in 2026 (all Vermont Senate seats are on the ballot every two years). Candidate filing opens April 27, 2026.

stateHouse.districtNote: Also referenced as Chittenden-12 in some sources. Covers part of South Burlington — verify your district at mvp.vermont.gov.

incumbents.name: Martin LaLonde
incumbents.bio: Martin LaLonde is a South Burlington attorney who has served in the Vermont House for several terms. He is known as a reliable Democratic vote and has been active on legal and judiciary issues.
notablePositions: Attorney by profession
Multi-term South Burlington representative
Active on legal and judiciary matters

stateHouse.districtNote: Covers part of South Burlington — verify your district at mvp.vermont.gov.

incumbents.name: Bridget Burkhardt
incumbents.bio: Bridget Burkhardt was elected to the Vermont House in 2024 and is serving her first term, representing a South Burlington district.
notablePositions: First-term representative (elected 2024)
South Burlington representative

stateHouse.districtNote: Covers part of South Burlington — verify your district at mvp.vermont.gov.

incumbents.name: Emilie Krasnow
incumbents.bio: Emilie Krasnow represents a South Burlington district in the Vermont House of Representatives.
notablePositions: South Burlington representative

stateHouse.districtNote: Covers part of South Burlington — verify your district at mvp.vermont.gov.

incumbents.name: Kate Nugent
incumbents.bio: Kate Nugent represents a South Burlington district in the Vermont House of Representatives.
notablePositions: South Burlington representative

stateHouse.districtNote: Covers part of South Burlington — verify your district at mvp.vermont.gov.

incumbents.name: Brian Minier
incumbents.bio: Brian Minier represents a South Burlington district in the Vermont House of Representatives.
notablePositions: South Burlington representative
